title: Alternative Materials For Hybrid Bonding
gist: Copper-to-copper hybrid bonding is becoming the interconnect of choice for AI-era chip density, but the standard copper/SiO2 recipe is hitting materials limits, pushing researchers toward alternative dielectrics (SiCN), passivating metals (silver, ruthenium), and compliant polymers (BCB) to manage thermal-expansion stress and surface contamination.

themes.theme: Why hybrid bonding is displacing solder bumps

insights.claim: As interconnect pitch shrinks, solder bumps become unreliable because there isn't enough material to form a robust connection, and copper-tin intermetallic compounds can raise resistance or cause mechanical failures, pushing manufacturers toward direct copper-copper hybrid bonding instead.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Equipment and materials suppliers serving solder/bump packaging face secular share loss to hybrid-bonding tooling as pitches scale down; watch bonder, CMP, and surface-activation equipment vendors for share gains.
insights.quote: As interconnect pitch is reduced, though, solder bumps become less reliable. There simply isn’t enough material to make a robust connection.

insights.claim: Hybrid bonding removes all intervening material between two chips, joining copper-to-copper and dielectric-to-dielectric directly, which reduces package thickness and maximizes interconnect density, and it has already scaled from initial 10 µm pitch down to demonstrated pitches as low as 400 nm at imec.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: The pitch roadmap (10 µm to 400 nm demonstrated) sets the technology runway investors can use to gauge how much further scaling headroom hybrid-bonding equipment and materials suppliers have before hitting fundamental limits.
insights.quote: Researchers at imec have demonstrated hybrid bonding with interconnect pitches as low as 400 nm.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Hybrid bonding, or direct copper-copper bonding'

insights.claim: Thermocompression bonding (TCB), the precursor technology that places copper studs on both bonding surfaces and fuses them with heat and pressure, becomes untenable at high interconnect densities because high temperatures and pressures cause interconnects to buckle and shift, degrading performance or causing outright failure.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: TCB-based packaging approaches have a ceiling; vendors and OSATs still reliant on TCB for advanced packaging face technology obsolescence risk as density requirements rise.

insights.claim: Successful hybrid bonding depends on creating a clean but chemically 'active' surface simultaneously on copper (which quickly forms a native oxide under ambient conditions) and on SiO2 (a stable, non-reactive dielectric), a fundamental chemical mismatch that drives most of the process complexity.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Any materials or equipment company solving the copper-oxide/SiO2-activation mismatch cheaply has a structural advantage in the hybrid-bonding supply chain; this is the recurring engineering problem across the whole sub-field.

insights.claim: Osaka University researchers (Hiroaki Tatsumi and colleagues) found in simulations that nanotwinned copper with exposed (111) planes, expected to enable faster surface diffusion for bonding, actually saw its twin boundaries stabilize the surface and prevent atomic rearrangement, whereas nanocrystalline copper with abundant grain boundaries showed successful void closure — an against-expectation result for a proposed bonding material.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Nanotwinned copper, often assumed to be a superior bonding surface, may not deliver on that promise; investors evaluating startups pitching nanotwinned-copper bonding IP should treat the claim skeptically pending further validation.
insights.locator: paragraph on Hiroaki Tatsumi, Osaka University, reference [2]

insights.claim: Plasma cleaning followed by bonding under vacuum achieves near-ideal, low-temperature bonding surfaces but is a relatively expensive solution because cleaning, alignment, and bonding must all occur without breaking vacuum, which is a cost driver for hybrid-bonding equipment.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Vacuum-integrated bonding clusters carry a cost premium versus atmospheric alternatives, a factor equipment buyers and toolmakers (e.g., bonder vendors) will weigh in capex decisions.
insights.locator: paragraph on plasma cleaning followed by vacuum bonding

insights.claim: Some research groups are shifting from self-assembled monolayer protection of copper toward passivating metal layers such as silver and ruthenium, which resist oxidation, remain conductive without degrading performance, and allow copper to diffuse through them to form a permanent bond.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Silver and ruthenium demand from advanced packaging is a secondary materials story worth tracking as hybrid bonding scales — specialty metals suppliers to the packaging industry could see incremental volume growth.
insights.locator: paragraph on self-assembled monolayers and passivating metals

insights.claim: The ideal hybrid-bonding topography has slightly recessed copper pads within a planar SiO2 surface so that thermal expansion during bonding closes the gap; too little copper dishing creates damaging stress from premature closure, while too much dishing leaves the gap between pads incompletely closed — making CMP control the critical yield lever.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: CMP equipment and process-control precision is a direct yield determinant for hybrid bonding, reinforcing the case for CMP toolmakers and metrology suppliers serving advanced packaging.

insights.claim: imec found that replacing SiO2 with the alternative dielectric SiCN gave higher bonding strength and better thermal stability, addressing the CTE mismatch problem that causes cracking and delamination in large copper features like TSVs under standard copper/SiO2 bonding.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: SiCN is emerging as a credible alternative dielectric for hybrid bonding; dielectric-deposition equipment and materials suppliers with SiCN capability (e.g., Lam Research per the companion article) stand to benefit from this shift.

insights.claim: Sukkyung Kang and colleagues found that precision CMP of partially cured benzocyclobutene (BCB) polymer is effectively impossible because abrasive slurry particles embed in the soft surface rather than being polished away, but an argon plasma treatment hardens the BCB surface enough to enable conventional polishing behavior via induced surface wrinkling that facilitates slurry flow.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Compliant-polymer bonding interfaces (BCB) require a specific plasma-hardening process step before CMP, an unresolved manufacturing detail that specialty process-equipment and materials vendors could productize.
insights.quote: Abrasive particles embed themselves in the soft polymer surface, where the polishing pad simply glides over them.
insights.locator: paragraph on Sukkyung Kang and BCB, reference [5]

insights.claim: Because BCB has a higher coefficient of thermal expansion than metallic copper (opposite the copper/SiO2 case), the desired bonding topography for BCB-based interfaces features a slightly recessed BCB layer relative to copper, the inverse of the standard copper-dishing target.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: Alternative dielectric/polymer bonding schemes each require bespoke process-control targets, meaning no single CMP recipe transfers across material systems — a complexity tax that favors incumbent equipment/process-IP leaders over new entrants.

insights.claim: The high-interconnect-density, low-thickness advantages of hybrid bonding are equally relevant to optical and RF devices built on non-silicon materials, meaning surface-cleanliness and topography challenges will recur as heterogeneous integration combines optics, logic, and memory in single packages.
insights.speaker: Katherine Derbyshire
insights.so_what: As silicon photonics and RF-on-package integration grow, hybrid-bonding materials science will need to generalize beyond copper/SiO2 systems, opening a longer-term addressable market for materials and process IP beyond pure-logic packaging.
